frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
generated_comment: See, people make this so complicated, but honestly it's quite simple in our house. I handle everything outside — the finances, the car, whenever something needs to be fixed or dealt with — and Priya handles the home side of things. It's not like I imposed anything on her, this is just how it naturally settled. She has a better eye for these things, she knows what the house needs, I frankly don't even know where half the stuff in our kitchen is kept. And her office timings are slightly more flexible than mine, so it just makes sense, no? Both of us are working, both of us are contributing — it's not like she's sitting at home. I respect that completely. But running a household also needs someone who's actually tracking it, and she does that very well. Why would I interfere in something she manages better than I ever could?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Rohit frames a deeply unequal division of labour as a natural, mutually arrived-at arrangement by invoking the language of complementary strengths and practicality, obscuring the structural asymmetry where he bears zero domestic cognitive load. The framing that his wife has a 'better eye' for domestic work naturalises gendered essentialism while the veneer of respect ('she's not sitting at home') insulates the statement from self-critique.
persona_text: You are Rohit. Age 33. Married for four years. A man in Kolkata. You and your wife both work. You have a domestic worker who comes in the morning and helps with cleaning. But cooking, grocery shopping, managing the domestic worker, planning meals, tracking what is running out in the kitchen Ñ all of this is your wife's domain. You have never cooked a meal in four years of marriage. You do not know the name of your regular vegetable vendor. You do not know what brand of dal you use. You are at a dinner party tonight and the conversation has turned to household division of labour. You are giving your opinion on why the current arrangement in your house is fair and practical and how it reflects both your natural strengths and your respective work pressures.
